title: Working with Individuals
text: citeme package provides collection helper functions managing individual contributor information across projects. functions help store, retrieve, reuse information people contribute work, ensuring consistency reducing repetitive data entry.

title: Working with Individuals
text: working multiple projects, often need cite contributors repeatedly. individual helper functions citeme allow : Build local database contributor information Reuse stored information across projects Maintain consistent contributor details Convert individual information different formats Add contributors various file types (DESCRIPTION, README, Quarto, Bookdown)

title: Working with Individuals
text: store_individuals() function extracts contributor information project saves local database future use. database stored user data directory persists across projects. function extracts information : R packages: Reads DESCRIPTION file get author details projects: Reads citation metadata files time store individuals, function tracks often person appears across projects. usage count helps prioritise frequently used contributors selecting individuals later.
code: library(citeme)  # Store individuals from a project with a DESCRIPTION file store_individuals("path/to/r-package")  # Store individuals from a project with citation metadata store_individuals("path/to/project")

title: Working with Individuals
text: select_individual() function allows interactively choose contributor stored database add new one. function presents stored individuals ordered : Usage count (frequently used first) Family name Given name ORCID identifier selecting interactively, can also: Add new individual database Update existing individual information
code: # Interactive selection from stored individuals individual <- select_individual()  # Quick selection by email address individual <- select_individual(email = "john.doe@example.com")  # Selection with specific language for affiliation # provided the individual's email matches an organisation with multilingual # names individual <- select_individual(lang = "nl-BE")

title: Working with Individuals
text: individual2person() function converts individual information R’s person object format, used DESCRIPTION files citation metadata. function automatically handles: Optional fields (email, ORCID, affiliation) Multiple roles per person Language-specific affiliations individual2badge() function creates Markdown badges displaying contributor information ORCID identifiers affiliations. useful README files documentation want display contributor information clickable links. individual2df() function converts person objects data frames, making easier work contributor information tabular format. function handles: Single person objects Lists person objects Missing fields (replaced empty strings) Multiple roles (combined comma-separated string)
code: # Convert with default author role person_obj <- individual2person(individual)  # Convert with specific role person_obj <- individual2person(individual, role = "cre")  # Convert with multiple roles person_obj <- individual2person(individual, role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Interactive selection and conversion person_obj <- individual2person(lang = "en-GB") # Create a badge for an individual badge_markdown <- individual2badge(individual)  # Result example: # [![ORCID: 0000-0000-0000-0000](https://img.shields.io/badge/ # ORCID-0000--0000--0000--0000-brightgreen)](https://orcid.org/ # 0000-0000-0000-0000) # Convert a person object to a data frame person_df <- individual2df(person_obj)  # The resulting data frame has columns: # - given: given name # - family: family name # - email: email address # - orcid: ORCID identifier # - ror: ROR identifier for affiliation # - affiliation: affiliation name # - role: role(s) as comma-separated string

title: Working with Individuals
text: individual2person() function converts individual information R’s person object format, used DESCRIPTION files citation metadata. function automatically handles: Optional fields (email, ORCID, affiliation) Multiple roles per person Language-specific affiliations
code: # Convert with default author role person_obj <- individual2person(individual)  # Convert with specific role person_obj <- individual2person(individual, role = "cre")  # Convert with multiple roles person_obj <- individual2person(individual, role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Interactive selection and conversion person_obj <- individual2person(lang = "en-GB")

title: Working with Individuals
text: individual2df() function converts person objects data frames, making easier work contributor information tabular format. function handles: Single person objects Lists person objects Missing fields (replaced empty strings) Multiple roles (combined comma-separated string)
code: # Convert a person object to a data frame person_df <- individual2df(person_obj)  # The resulting data frame has columns: # - given: given name # - family: family name # - email: email address # - orcid: ORCID identifier # - ror: ROR identifier for affiliation # - affiliation: affiliation name # - role: role(s) as comma-separated string

title: Working with Individuals
text: add_individual() function provides unified interface adding contributors various file types project. Supported file types: DESCRIPTION files: R package metadata Quarto files: _quarto.yml *.qmd documents README files: README.md README.Rmd Bookdown index files: index.md index.Rmd function automatically: Detects file type Prompts individual selection Adds individual specified role(s) Updates file place R packages (DESCRIPTION files), standard R CMD check roles supported: "aut": Author "cre": Creator/Maintainer "ctb": Contributor "rev": Reviewer "cph": Copyright holder "fnd": Funder "pbl": Publisher Quarto Bookdown documents, roles mapped YAML fields: "aut" -> author field "rev" -> reviewer field "cph" -> rightsholder field "fnd" -> funder field "pbl" -> publisher field
code: # Add an author to a DESCRIPTION file add_individual("path/to/DESCRIPTION", role = "aut")  # Add a maintainer to an R package add_individual("path/to/package", role = "cre")  # Add multiple roles add_individual("path/to/package", role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Add to a Quarto document add_individual("path/to/document.qmd", role = "aut")  # Add a reviewer to a Quarto document add_individual("path/to/document.qmd", role = "rev")

what: Role Mapping for Different File Types
title: Working with Individuals
text: R packages (DESCRIPTION files), standard R CMD check roles supported: "aut": Author "cre": Creator/Maintainer "ctb": Contributor "rev": Reviewer "cph": Copyright holder "fnd": Funder "pbl": Publisher Quarto Bookdown documents, roles mapped YAML fields: "aut" -> author field "rev" -> reviewer field "cph" -> rightsholder field "fnd" -> funder field "pbl" -> publisher field

title: Working with Individuals
text: has_person_role() function checks whether person object specific roles. select_person_role() function helps interactively specify roles contributor.
code: # Check if person has author role has_person_role(person_obj, role = "aut")  # Check for multiple roles has_person_role(person_obj, role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Returns TRUE if the person has at least one of the specified roles # Interactive role selection roles <- select_person_role()  # The function presents common roles and allows you to select one or more: # - Author # - Creator (Maintainer) # - Contributor # - Reviewer # - Copyright holder # - Funder # - Publisher

title: Working with Individuals
text: has_person_role() function checks whether person object specific roles.
code: # Check if person has author role has_person_role(person_obj, role = "aut")  # Check for multiple roles has_person_role(person_obj, role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Returns TRUE if the person has at least one of the specified roles

title: Working with Individuals
text: select_person_role() function helps interactively specify roles contributor.
code: # Interactive role selection roles <- select_person_role()  # The function presents common roles and allows you to select one or more: # - Author # - Creator (Maintainer) # - Contributor # - Reviewer # - Copyright holder # - Funder # - Publisher

title: Working with Individuals
text: typical workflow managing individuals across projects:
code: library(citeme)  # Step 1: Store individuals from existing projects store_individuals("projects/my-r-package") store_individuals("projects/another-project")  # Step 2: Start a new project and add contributors # The function will prompt you to select from stored individuals add_individual("new-project/DESCRIPTION", role = c("aut", "cre"))  # Step 3: Add more contributors as needed add_individual("new-project/DESCRIPTION", role = "ctb")  # Step 4: Store the new project's individuals for future use store_individuals("new-project")

title: Working with Individuals
text: individual helper functions support multilingual affiliations lang parameter. relevant individual member known organisation (see vignette("organisation")) names multiple languages. organisation names multiple languages, can specify language use: language code follow BCP 47 standard (e.g. "en-GB", "nl-", "fr-FR").
code: # Select individual with English affiliation individual_en <- select_individual(lang = "en-GB")  # Select individual with Dutch affiliation individual_nl <- select_individual(lang = "nl-BE")  # The affiliation field will use the specified language when available person_en <- individual2person(lang = "en-GB")

title: Working with Individuals
text: Build database early: Run store_individuals() existing projects build comprehensive database contributors. Please note store_individuals() extracts information projects contains metadata format one citeme writes. Use email quick lookups: know contributor’s email, use select_individual(email = "...") avoid interactive selection. Keep information updated: contributor details change (e.g. new affiliation), update prompted select_individual(). Store project: Run store_individuals() adding contributors new project keep database current. consistent roles: Use standard R CMD check roles packages appropriate roles document types.

title: Interactive Input and Validation
text: citeme package provides functions interactive user input validation common data types used citation metadata. functions ensure data quality validating user input established formats standards.

title: Interactive Input and Validation
text: ask_yes_no() function provides simple interface yes/questions validation. function: Wraps utils::askYesNo() additional validation Repeats question valid answer given Returns default value non-interactive sessions Handles invalid input gracefully informative warnings ask_orcid() function prompts ORCID iD, unique persistent identifier researchers. function: Validates ORCID format using validate_orcid() Allows empty strings optional ORCID identifiers Repeats prompt valid input provided Checks format checksum validity ask_ror() function prompts ROR identifier, uniquely identifies research organisations. function: Validates ROR format using validate_ror() Ensures identifier starts https://ror.org/ Checks checksum component Repeats prompt valid input provided ask_email() function prompts email address format validation. function: Validates email format using validate_email() Checks proper structure (local@domain) verify whether address exists Repeats prompt valid format provided ask_url() function prompts URL validation. function: Validates URL format using validate_url() Requires http:// https:// prefix Repeats prompt valid input provided ask_language() function prompts language code following BCP 47 standard. function: Validates BCP 47 standard using validate_language() Accepts codes like "en-GB", "nl-", "fr-FR" Repeats prompt valid input provided ask_new_license() function helps select add license project. menu_first() function provides interactive menu first option default. used non-interactive session, returns index first option.
code: library(citeme) # Ask a yes/no question with default answer answer <- ask_yes_no("Do you want to continue?", default = TRUE)  # Custom prompts answer <- ask_yes_no(   "Overwrite existing file?",   default = FALSE,   prompts = c("Yes", "No", "Cancel") ) # Ask for an ORCID iD orcid <- ask_orcid()  # Custom prompt orcid <- ask_orcid(prompt = "Enter your ORCID: ")  # Empty strings are allowed (optional ORCID) # Valid format: 0000-0000-0000-0000 (last digit can be X) # Ask for a ROR identifier ror <- ask_ror()  # Valid format: https://ror.org/0abcd1234 # Ask for an email address email <- ask_email()  # Custom prompt email <- ask_email(prompt = "Contact email: ") # Ask for a URL url <- ask_url("Please enter the website URL: ")  # The URL must start with http:// or https:// # Ask for a language code # The list of options is based on the languages used in the `org_list` object # The user can add another language code if needed lang <- ask_language(org = inbo_org_list()) # Interactive license selection org <- inbo_org_list() license <- ask_new_license(org$get_listed_licenses)  # The function: # 1. Lists available licenses # 2. Allows selection or addition of new license # 3. Validates the selection # Create a menu choices <- c("Option A", "Option B", "Option C") selection <- menu_first(choices, title = "Select an option:")  # Returns the index of the selected option # The first option is highlighted as default

title: Interactive Input and Validation
text: validation functions return logical vectors indicating whether element passes validation. designed work vectors, making suitable batch validation. validate_orcid() function checks format checksum ORCID identifiers. function: Checks format: 0000-0000-0000-0000 (last digit can X) Validates checksum using modulo 11 algorithm Accepts empty strings Works character vectors validate_ror() function checks ROR identifier format checksum. function: Validates identifier structure Checks checksum component Works character vectors validate_email() function checks email address format. function: Uses comprehensive regex pattern https://emailregex.com Checks local domain parts Handles complex email formats verify whether addresses exist Works character vectors validate_url() function checks URL format. function: Requires http:// https:// prefix Works string verify whether URLs accessible validate_language() function checks language codes BCP 47 standard. function: Validates BCP 47 standard Accepts language-region combinations Works character vectors
code: # Validate ORCID identifiers orcids <- c(   "0000-0001-2345-6789",   "0000-0002-3456-789X",   "invalid-orcid",   "" ) validate_orcid(orcids) [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE # Validate ROR identifiers validate_ror("02catss52") [1] TRUE validate_ror("https://ror.org/02catss52") [1] FALSE validate_ror("not-a-ror") [1] FALSE # Validate email addresses emails <- c(   "user@example.com",   "firstname.lastname@domain.co.uk",   "invalid.email",   "@example.com" ) validate_email(emails) [1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE # Returns logical vector indicating validity # Validate URLs validate_url("https://example.com") [1] TRUE validate_url("http://subdomain.example.org/path") [1] TRUE validate_url("ftp://example.com") # Invalid (ftp not allowed) [1] FALSE validate_url("not-a-url") [1] FALSE # Validate language codes validate_language("en-GB") [1] "en-GB" validate_language("nl-BE") [1] "nl-BE" validate_language("en") Error: ! `language` must be in xx-YY format. e.g. 'en-GB', 'nl-BE' validate_language("invalid") Error: ! `language` must be in xx-YY format. e.g. 'en-GB', 'nl-BE' # Returns logical vector indicating validity

text: Use validation functions early: Validate input soon possible provide immediate feedback. Batch validation: Use validation functions vectors efficient data cleaning. Non-interactive fallbacks: Always provide fallbacks non-interactive sessions. Combine validators: Use multiple validation functions complex data requirements.

code: # Pattern: Validate before storing if (interactive()) {   email <- ask_email() } else {   email <- config$email } stopifnot("Invalid email" = validate_email(email)) # Pattern: Clean and filter data frame data$valid_orcid <- validate_orcid(data$orcid) data$valid_email <- validate_email(data$email)  clean_data <- data[data$valid_orcid & data$valid_email, ] # Pattern: Handle optional ORCID orcid <- ask_orcid() # Empty string is valid if (orcid != "") {   # Use ORCID   person_comment <- c(ORCID = orcid) } # Pattern: Validate and report issues contributors <- read.csv("contributors.csv") invalid_emails <- contributors$email[!validate_email(contributors$email)]  if (length(invalid_emails) > 0) {   warning(     "Invalid emails found:\n",     paste(invalid_emails, collapse = "\n")   ) }

what: Why bother setting up an org_list?
title: Organisations
text: citeme uses org_list check whether project complies organisation’s rules naming conventions, copyright holders, funders, publishers. allows organisation enforce consistent set rules across projects.

title: Organisations
text: org_item class stores information single organisation. requires two mandatory fields: name email. name must named character vector name BCP 47 language code (e.g. "en-GB", "nl-"). allows organisation name used different languages. first entry used default matching language available. gives users contact point organisation individual authors unavailable. citeme uses email domain detect whether person affiliated organisation. Optionally can provide: ROR identifier (ror) website URL (website) logo URL (logo) Whether affiliated persons must ORCID identifier (orcid) Zenodo community identifier (zenodo) Allowed licenses per project type (license) org_item defines required status organisation three roles: rightsholder, funder, publisher. accepts one following values: default three roles "optional".

title: Organisations
text: org_item defines required status organisation three roles: rightsholder, funder, publisher. accepts one following values: default three roles "optional".

title: Organisations
text: org_list class holds collection org_item objects. rules different items must mutually compatible. example, one organisation rightsholder = "single", organisation can set "single" "shared" role. org_list also stores URL organisation’s git host. citeme looks organisation.yml file citeme repository URL retrieve minimum rules organisation. example, git = "https://github.com/inbo", citeme looks https://github.com/inbo/citeme.

title: Organisations
text: Use org_item$new() create org_item objects. Note license values must URLs pointing publicly accessible Markdown files license text. recommend hosting files organisation’s citeme repository. citeme caches license files locally includes LICENSE.md packages projects. combine items org_list: final step store org_list citeme repository. write() creates organisation.yml file given directory. Commit push file remote repository.

what: Getting the default org_list for a project

text: Run get_default_org_list() inside git project fetch default organisation list organisation’s citeme repository. function uses origin remote project determine organisation look . get_default_org_list() stores result citeme’s user-level configuration. need run per organisation, organisation’s defaults change. , org_list$new()$read() returns cached information.
code: # Set up a demo git repository test_dir <- tempfile("test") dir.create(test_dir) library(gert) git_init(test_dir) git_remote_add(   url = "https://github.com/inbo/test",   name = "origin",   repo = test_dir ) # Fetch the default organisation list from https://github.com/inbo/citeme my_org <- get_default_org_list(test_dir) my_org

title: The org_item R6 class — org_item
text: name named vector organisation names one languages. first item vector default language. names vector must match language code. email email address organisation. Used contact organisation. used detect person affiliated organisation. orcid Whether organisation requires ORCID every person uses organisation affiliation. rightsholder required copyright holder status organisation. "optional" means organisation required copyright holder. "single" means organisation must copyright holder. "shared" means organisation must one copyright holders. "" means copyright holder specified, organisation must copyright holder. funder required funder status organisation. categories rightsholder. publisher required publisher status organisation. categories rightsholder. license list allowed licenses organisation. list may contain following items: package, project, data. Every item must named character vector allowed licenses. names must match license name. values must either match path license template checklist package absolute URL publicly available markdown file license text. Use character(0) indicate organisation require specific license item. package defaults c("GPL-3.0", "MIT"). project defaults "CC 4.0". data defaults "CC0 1.0". ror optional ROR ID organisation. zenodo optional Zenodo community ID organisation. website optional website URL organisation. logo optional logo URL organisation.
